title: 2025/12/02 - EDSI (Part 1)
displayTitle: 2025-12-02 - Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee - 2024/2025 Annual review of New Zealand Post Limited [Part 1]
committee: Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee
topic: 2024/2025 Annual review of New Zealand Post Limited
description: Tuesday 2 December 2025 | Economic Development Science and Innovation Committee 2024/2025 Annual review of New Zealand Post Limited Hearing of evidence: New Zealand Post Limited Dame Paula Rebstock, Chair NZ Post David Walsh, Chief Executive Officer Rhonda Richardson, Chief Financial Officer Malcolm Shaw, Chief Governance and Sustainability Officer Greta Parker, External Communications Lead Matthew Kenning, Government Relations Advisor
descriptionSummary: 2024/2025 Annual review of New Zealand Post Limited. New Zealand Post Limited. Dame Paula Rebstock, Chair NZ Post

chunks.text: Thank you so much. Uh, we have one hour with you, so, uh, if you would like to start with some opening remarks and then our members will have questions. Thank you. Well, and thank you, um, for the opportunity to come today and attend the hearing and answer questions and hopefully give you some background on, um, New Zealand post.

Um, it's my first select committee hearing as Chair of Zel Post. So, um, um, I'm particularly grateful to have with me today David Walsh, who's our longstanding chief executive. So in, uh, full year 25 New Zealand Post reported a loss of 2 million, um, that was 12 million short of plan, um, but it was also 12 million improvement on the previous financial year and a 54 million lift over the last two years.

Behind this result were par parcel volumes of 88 million, up from 84 million in full year 24, supported by the purchase of the BPT customer base. The softer economic cycle, uh, made for a tougher year than planned, I think goes without saying.

And we responded through cost management, which we have continued to improve our cost structures in order to, um, improve performance in the current year. Um, financial performance of our Flyway and supply chain solutions investments has also improved.

And you may be aware that in the first few years post COVID, um, we were behind plan on those investments. But that is, um, coming through strongly now and continued into the current year. Our infrastructure investment program to build a scale scalable modern parcel network is now complete.

The Auckland Processing Center in weary and in and our new automated delivery depot at Mount Rosco, our fully operational and performing well, they, um, really are something to behold. So anytime you have the opportunity to visit our sites, we would warmly welcome you because it's truly a multi-generational investment that has been made in the future of New Zealand Post.

We continue to operate a commercially sustainable mail service. Mail volumes continue to decline with 158 million letters delivered in full year 25. Um, that's down from 187 million in the previous financial year. And that's part of a long-term decline that we see across every jurisdiction in mail and, um, presents its own challenges and opportunities in terms of our future business strategy.

We continue to balance mail pricing with service and cost management to achieve acceptable margins and meet expectations of our customers to ensure we can continue to effectively respond to future volume reductions. The government has recently signed with New Zealand Post an amended deed of understanding.

This provides New Zealand Post with urban mail delivery flexibility, um, from three days to two days per week. Rural mail delivery flexibility from five days to three days per week, and a reduction in the minimum number of retail points of presence from 880 to 500 initially.

Our first priority is to make changes to the urban retail network to ensure we have the right number of stores in the right locations while providing quality customer service. For example, we recently opened a new retail hub in New Market Auckland, and we're planning to open more of these retail hubs that will be complemented, um, with our retail partner network.

Now, again, this is a great site to go to see. So while we are going to close some postal shops, um, that have low usage, um, we do intend to upgrade, um, many of the ones that we will keep into the future to ensure our customers have a much better customer experience.

Right now, New Zealand post is in the busiest season of the year. The auditor who looks after New Zealand post out there said, you're very busy delivering Christmas, and I thought that was absolutely right. We are very busy delivering Christmas and um, thought it was a delightful way to think of the role that post plays in the community.

We delivered 2.3 million parcels last week and are predicting to deliver 2.4 million parcels this week. Wow. And just to give you a sense of what happens with Black Friday sales, um, yesterday we had a, uh, we processed over a half a million parcels.

Wow. So it was the second highest day, um, that we've experienced, and we are expecting this to continue now through this period. We're incredibly proud to support all of our customers, including our newest customer, ikea, um, with our unmatched networked maximize trading, um, during the festive holiday season.

Looking ahead, we expect to continue to improve our commercial performance. We remain focused on cost containment, growing the business, and being a regular dividend payer over the coming here, I'm very happy to take Thank you questions. So we go first to Thank you very much for coming in, in this really busy period.

I have some questions for you about, um, the costs for consumers and also the competition that you're seeing in your market. Um, and then if I get to return, um, I'll ask you about workforce issues. Um, so thinking about these stamps, they're now almost $3. Um, New Zealand Post has chosen to put that up twice in the last two years, and what's cost inflation for around 25%.

How are you thinking about those price increases for small businesses and consumers, particularly older Kiwis, who rely on those connections as vital connective services that are of a public good? Yeah, I, I guess it's one of the hardest decisions right, in the face of declining volumes, and we do expect that decline to continue.

Um, but under the current deed, um, New Zealand Post, um, will no longer receive any support for maintaining its network. So we have to do that in a financially sustainable way. So To, so what impact has the, the minister's expectations that New Zealand Post will prioritize dividends to the government played into those cost increases for consumers?

You know, we, we right now are trying to make sure that the postal side letter side can cover, you know, can cover its cost and maintain a reasonable margin. We don't, you know, our ability in the future to grow dividends is gonna very much be driven by the growth part of the business, which is parcels.

There's no question about it. And interestingly, I think in the medium term, once the number of letters drops to a certain level, we'll see the ability to deliver through the parcel network and you'll actually see those costs come down and some point in the future as well as the level of service will go potentially up again.

So I, you know, it's, it's a really hard transition, I think is the honest truth, both in terms of price and, and the, um, how, how often deliveries are made, but we're doing our very best to manage it as well as we can both for our customers, also for the shareholder.

Um, I think we're doing a remarkable job if you compare how this is fairing compared to other jurisdictions, and I think we've got a great medium term plan on how we can continue to meet the need of customers. Doesn't mean higher prices for stamps, I'm afraid it does. And it, you know, that picture doesn't change for a few years. Um, I hope I can come back to the affordability point, but, um, just something you've said there really sparks my interest around what you see the market dynamics playing out around this.

Um, you have a competitor, um, who is busily trying to make market share against your market share. Um, it is, do we have a problem here in New Zealand's market where we might end up with New Zealand post offering those vital connections to communities that it can no longer cross subsidize if it loses more market share to a mix?

Well, a mix, um, is competing with us on the parcel side. Mm-hmm. So we do have competition on the letter side in the urban areas. And, you know, that presents challenges to us. I'm not going to pretend it doesn't, because we have a, we have under the deed a commitment to provide, um, service to the whole country.

They don't need to do that. They can cherry pick those areas where the return is highest. Exactly. Um, does that, um, uh, and I'm not saying that to plead for, you know, it's not a special pleading at all because we think we've got a means to deal with that. And in the future, actually it will be important to supporting our rural network anyway.

So, you know, that that is the case. We face competition both on the letter and the parcel side. Um, the parcel side, if that's what you were asking about, the competitive environment, anytime the economy is soft, you're going to see intense competition margins will probably get squeezed.

Everyone will compete hard for whatever business is there. And that's exactly what we've seen. And we are really confident that with our new investments, that makes us more competitive on the cost side, that we are competing and winning in the marketplace. We're both retaining customers in the parcel business and we're gaining customers.

And IKEA is a great example. We got part of that business and our new investments, you know, the processing center in Auckland is world leading. And, you know, there's no doubt that that will help us to, um, meet, um, our own expectations that of the community and the shareholder in the future.

I guess, um, the, my question about the, there is this book of 10 stamps now costs about $30, which is fine for an MP sending leaders to constituents, but isn't fine for those small businesses who are wearing that and then passing it back to the consumers or those, those older New Zealanders who rely on post, are they sending fewer packages, fewer leaders, um, and will that have a long term impact on New Zealand post's ability to compete with than a competitor that might change the market dynamics later on?

There's a long term decline in people sending letters everywhere in the world. We're no different. Um, and that will continue. And I mean, in the most recent period, as an example, we did the local election mailouts. Mm-hmm. That was, it's very important to New Zealand post, but in the future that will go, it's a matter of time, not our decision, but you see it happening everywhere.

The banks, insurance companies, everyone's moving to digital mailouts. So we will see a long-term decline. And the hardest decision for us is to judge what that decline is going to be just because of the transition to digital mail, um, and do enough to cover our costs and continue to provide a service to those who don't have another option, but not do it not increase the prices so much that we actually speed up the transition away from most.

Is that happening? Well, there's no way to know. Um, I think in the past we probably were slow to respond potentially to the decline, but, you know, it's, you just don't know. And I mean, I'm an economist by training. I last board meeting said to them, can't we do some on a metric analysis to try to understand what the elasticity is here so we know whether we're just doing enough to maintain the service and, and hitting it matching the natural decline.

Or are we actually pricing in a way that is accelerating and the we just can't do that? Um, I'm afraid, I mean, I was very keen to a answer the same question and, uh, I accept that that can't be done. Yeah. Yeah. Uh, supplementary from Dr. David, um, You mentioned that, uh, rural mail will go from five times a week to three times a week, and, uh, I think the rural constituents will be really interested to hear how you will be managing that dynamic in relation to small parcels as well.

So what's the interaction between the two and, um, how will you maintain that public good service that we've all come to recognize and appreciate? I think a lot, some of what you have to remember on these changes is we, we are allowed to go up to that amount when and when we do is will be driven by the business dynamics.

So, uh, I think that's really important to understand. But the dynamic between the mail and the parcel delivery days, the deed has stipulated that for the letters that we, we can go down to three. It doesn't mean that we will only deliver parcels for that number of days.

And I'll just let Dave briefly comment on that. Yeah, sure. Yeah. The, the D talks about the minimum of rural delivery frequency, but primarily what drives our rural network is parcel demand and parcel demand. And much of the retail of rural network is still very strong. So we still expect for the largest majority of the rural network will continue to live five days a week.

We may only inject mail into it three days in some cases, but, uh, there will be some parts of the network over time will make a decision on can that other network sustain five days of parcel delivery. But I think right now it's not the highest order of, uh, priority for us. Most of our rural delivery will continue as you see it today.

Mm-hmm. And so the system optimization between the two forms is what I'm hearing? Yeah. Yeah. So we'll, we'll continue to, uh, just, uh, monitor that demand for male services. Uh, in fact, uh, this year we've seen, uh, our rural demand for parcel delivers be stronger, uh, growth than we've seen in urban museum.

Uh, so, uh, yeah, I know how well regarded the rural delivery drivers are in, uh, rural New Zealand. They do a fantastic job for us. Uh, but uh, as I mentioned right now, uh, they are doing in a pretty full delivering parcel, uh, demand at the moment. If there are communities that are affected, uh, when we need to re-look at this, we will of course, uh, engage with those communities and, and, uh, advise 'em what we're thinking about. Thank you. Mm-hmm.

A new brand from Barbara. Uh, thank you so much. Um, oh, first of all, just acknowledge the right, honorable Jim Bulger, who was a couple of cheers before you. I mean, I know a lot of New Zealand posters are for life, so I I appreciate some of your staff may have worked with him since then. Um, just looking at more your financial results, despite an improvement of 12 million off your forecasted amount, were you surprised by s and p downgrading your long term issuer?

I think it was credit rating. Can you repeat that? Yeah. Uh, so, um, the s and p rating, uh, reflects the, the balance sheet strength. Mm-hmm. And particularly our, uh, uh, key ratios on, on interest coverage, uh, payment coverage. So, uh, we, we've got a reasonably good balance sheet, uh, but we also knew, uh, the, the board got us to go through a, a capital adequacy exercise on the balance sheet.

So we did have reasonable capacity at that stage to, uh, determine whether it was appropriate to make a special dividend or not. That special dividend has some impacts, of course, in terms of your, um, uh, interest cost line and aspects of that. So, uh, we were comfortable with the process. The board took, uh, a very due consideration of that.

Uh, we were aware that s and p would, uh, consider the impact that that would have on our rating. Uh, and as a result they have, uh, dropped us a couple of notches. So as a result, but in terms of, uh, you know, it's something we're aware of, but it's something we can, we can manage and, And no real significant practical implications from that other than borrowing costs in the Future. Yeah. There may be a little bit of marginal cost in it, but that's well within now, But you can manage it. Yeah.

Ability to manage. Yep. So, sorry. And then dang, Paula, and just your opening comments, you talked about, um, despite some of the branch closures, you're looking at retail hubs. I mean, some of our communities are, are thinking Nelson, dun, need and Christchurch, you know, mps have said there've been a number of closures there.

Can you assure the public where that these retail hubs will be in their communities or where they can go to? Will it be, you know, within an hour's drive? Will it be within 15 minutes? I mean, I'm just with the branches of closing and you're gonna do retail hubs, will they be able to access them? Which I fact that Yeah, I'll let David answer that Operation. Yeah. Yeah.

So there, there, there is quite a, uh, extensive exercise that the team have done. Uh, as we mentioned earlier, our footprint is actually very, very big. And uh, I think if we, compared to other retailers, we'd have one of the biggest, uh, footprints in the, in the country. So we have gone through a process of wanting to, uh, we've done a lot of mapping around areas about accessibility and, uh, size of our, uh, retail footprint in some areas.

Uh, like we're building one in Palmerston North at the moment there as well, a hub similar to what we have built in new market. And that will be complimented with agency partners in various locations. We've got certain targets around accessibility and certain, uh, parameters to access and percentage of population near to sites.

I don't have all that detail with me right now, but we can share it. But it has been quite systematically work through and just to get a balance between a footprint that we can afford to run as well as high quality access for New Zealanders that want to access it. I think Worth reextend the invitation to mps to meet with us on what's happening in their own, um, areas.

Um, we're very happy to do that. Um, and, and hopefully to give more confidence around it and there will be ongoing consultation, so Thank you for that offer offer. And yeah, I mean, I mean, I probably say the labor cus would wee actually you come and brief us just the cold workers that way you get all the mps at the same time.

So just a supplementary of that. Um, you'll provide us with obviously the analysis that you came back to. I mean, the other, the other question that I had was, what is, um, will there be public consultation? Not just, I mean obviously mps represent communities, but will there be wider public consultation? Yeah, we, we See, how will they know? Yeah, So right now we are engaging with our, um, our agency partners, uh, to give them, uh, some advice about some areas where we would not be able to maintain that agency partnership.

And they're generally ones with very, very low volume, uh, going through them. The ones that we're talking to at the moment that count for about 11 or 12% of our, of our footprint, but only about one and a half to 2% of our total volume and revenue going through it. So we've gotta be quite realistic and practical about how that works. We are then engaging with another group of agent partners just with an expression of interest to see which ones we'd like to continue to partner with us.

And once we've got that feedback later on this year, we'll make some decisions about which locations we're able to maintain or change in terms of the communities. We will have a very proactive advice about what changes are planned and where they'll get access, uh, to new sites as they as new footprint develops. So, so that's, uh, the process we'll be following. Yeah, I just highly recommend you engage with those groups like Gray Power, et cetera, for the, our seniors who will find access difficult.

Thank you. Alright, a supplementary Thank you. Just rounding off this question about, um, your ratings. Um, has the Supreme Court's decision in the Uber case had an impact on the way that your ratings agency have viewed your position? It's not had an impact date, but obviously we'll be watching it, um, carefully. Okay.

Yeah. Mm-hmm. Um, I do wanna ask about that a bit more generally. Um, so we know that we have now in New Zealand a position where, um, courier drivers potentially, um, uh, will, will be considered employees. How are you working through that as a business? Will it change the way that New Zealand post deals with its couriers? Um, what does that practically mean on the ground?

I, I think, you know, the, the situation for New Zealand Post it's, we'd love to discuss it, tell, but we do have a couple of cases before the courts, so really not in a position that we can discuss the ins and outs of that decision and its relevance or otherwise for New Zealand Post.

And, you know, we will respect the findings of the court at the relevant time. Yeah. Alright. No, thank you. Uh, new question from Scott Willis. No, no, no. Scott will Primely, Everyone knows the, uh, the electric scooters that deliver post and that's a fantastic example of, of leadership in reducing carbon emissions.

But you've signaled here that you're stepping away from the Science-Based Targets initiative. Um, I'm interested to understand why, and I can, you know, I've got a good sense of why, but if you could explain in your own words why you have decided to move away from that initiative.

Yeah, really happy to have the chance to address that because we are deeply committed to, um, decarbonization and our part in it, and we continue to do the things that we have planned to do to move us in the right direction. But when the decision was first, um, taken to move to science-based targets, some of those assumptions didn't actually turn out to be what we thought they were.

And frankly, you know, it could cost us as much as a hundred million dollars to move there, try to move there now. And it's just simply beyond us. And even beyond that, there are other constraints that we can't meet at this time. So the board and management's position is we want to move back to that as soon as we practically can.

Um, but that, um, we also don't want to pretend right now we're doing something we simply don't have the means to do. And so, um, what we are focused on is doing the things we can both within our own network to electrify our own fleet, but also working with some of our partner, um, partners to encourage them to do the same and we're having some significant success.

Um, the third leg of our strategy was to work on the, the larger delivery vehicles. That's proving if I'm perfectly open with you, to be harder than we expected. Um, so right now we're probably doubling down on the other two limbs and we'll come back to that one too, when hydrogen and other options become, uh, you know, a, a closer substitute for us.

So I wouldn't want you to think that we've done that because we've changed our view on our commitment to decarbonization. We have not, um, it's just simply at this time we're not able to move in the direction that we had hoped to be able to move in, but we will be looking for the right time and opport to move back to a, to to that strategy.

So, um, your movement towards your, your light vehicle fleet or decarbonizing your light vehicle fleet, which is one of the easier things to do, you, you've moved that out another two years. Um, is that, is that simply because of increased cost or is there, is there that thing in Play? Yeah, there are a couple of aspects.

It cost is we my primary driver, but uh, our corporate own right fleet, we've moved that along a long way. Uh, and uh, we're getting very close to, uh, getting that through, uh, a, a full change. The contract partners that Paul was referring to, we, we slowed that down a little bit there, there was both access to, uh, appropriate vehicles as well as, uh, the contract partner's confidence to pick that vehicle up, uh, for their own use.

As Paula mentioned. Uh, so far this year we've seen a really good pickup of that. About 50 of our contract partners, uh, this year, uh, this financial year alone have converted to EV vehicles. So, uh, we'd like to keep that momentum going and that will balance between how much we can help that transition versus the general availability and economics of running those.

But, uh, on the whole, uh, you know, I've spoken to some of our contract partners delivering an EV vehicles. There are a lot of reasons why they like them, but their maintenance costs are lower and their daily fuel costs are lower, but normally their first capital cost is a bit higher. So that, that's what we're working our way through. Okay. And just One another supplementary supplement supplementary.

So I mean, I, I agree with you. Once you've driven an ev you're not gonna go back to a, a fossil vehicle vehicle, but, um, in a more difficult economic environment, what exactly are you doing with your contracted, um, partners to enable them? Are you providing them with information?

Are you providing them with, uh, an, um, some sort of support Yep. To, to invest or, or a longer term contract? What, what is it that you're doing? It's almost all of the above actually. Yeah. So in terms of, uh, we are working with our delivery partners on the normal cycle of vehicle replacement.

So we get a sense of what's due to be replaced. We have had a small incentive program in place, uh, where we've supported the position of that. We spent about half a million dollars a year to support that, uh, transition of vehicles. Uh, and uh, each year we reset that budget both on affordability and, uh, interest, uh, in the contract partners to do it.

So as you said, this year has been a very good year in terms of take up of that a cliff. Alright. A supplementary from Barbara. Yeah, sorry. So a supplementary of, um, Scott's question, just on page 47 of your annual report, it just talked about the facility with, uh, SCF Lead, low Emissions Delivery Limited is SCCF left below Emissions Delivery Limited, are they a separate company or is clearly not part of the group?

I just want to check if they're a separate company that you contract with for that loan facility. C Uh, I will check on the detail of that. I expect that, uh, we had a partnership with a, uh, we sending out a partnership through, uh, the, um, green Investment Fund and, uh, possibly that as a conversion of the Green Investment Fund group, but I'll come back with the specifics of that.

So we did have a lease partnership Tructure Yeah. That supported the transition, uh, into, um, uh, Lower mission S contract partners for lower mission. I, I assume, sorry, I shouldn't have the detail. No, That's all right. No, that's, thank you. Alright. Um, new, Um, and just wanna clarify Madam Chair, um, were you rolling my previous primary question out or? Um, no, So yeah, so I just wanted to go to Scott Willis.

You can start a new primary. Great. Um, just returning to the workforce issues, um, think that is an area where people will be very interested in. Um, one question I have is why is there not, it, it seems that there isn't a contingency set aside for the liability associated with the Uber decision. Why is that and what is the board considering the potential size of that liability to be?

No, we have no, we have no, uh, liability put aside for that. Okay. So, Yeah, I guess, um, why I'm asking is, um, helpfully Dam Paul, you've set out that these are ongoing issues that, um, both first union and, um, I believe we do have raised with the company over many years.

So you might expect that there are some outstanding legal issues to be resolved with the board interested in. If that's not a contingency, then how should, um, the members of the New Zealand public sort of look at this, the size of the potential, um, for this to have an impact on not only the New Zealand post, but the entire sort of postal and career services that we we use in this country? Yeah.

Uh, we, we do not have a contingent liability put aside. I mean, at this point in time, and you're probably aware, there were statements in the judgment about that, those findings being specific to those facts. And so the interesting question going forward is, are those facts present in circumstances that affect New Zealand Post?

And, you know, and forming a view on whether we need to put aside a contingent liability, we'd be taking into account our own legal advice. So, right. That I really can't say more than that. Yeah. So can I say members that we don't get too speculative here? No. So yeah, just, uh, this is our opportunity to, um, assist them on their performance.

Can I ask not speculating stuff can a by as long as it's not speculating. Yeah, no, no. It's just to clarify. So you were saying though, contingent liability for these financial statements or any, or for the future in your sense? Well, Oh, that's to be determined. Yeah. Yeah, That's fine. That's fine. If there's a change on information. Yeah, I was Justing just Clarifying if there's a change in information, then we would consider that, but the Supreme Court findings came out subsequent to these, so of course we will revisit that for each

and every set of accounts. Yeah, I just would've thought for accounting purposes probably would've come under employee expenses if, if in the future the court sites. Yeah. Alright. Uh, you Have a new, Yeah. Uh, just a, just a clarification around, um, what I hope I'm understanding correctly. So New Zealand Post hasn't done anything in the business to recognize, um, workers who might be potentially employees at all? There's no, could They have already answered that question?

So if you, do you have a new primary? No, I'm not sure they have. No, they have, I've heard, um, I've heard this Live until, until there's a finding that that is, that's what they said before the case. There's not anything for us yet to do. Okay. And, but as I said, you, we will, you know, clearly New Zealand Post will comply with any legal findings that occur in the future. Uh, It's new primary Scott Willis. Uh, It's not exactly new, it's just a supplementary.

So, um, I'll have to, um, based on, so you know what, what I'm saying here. So as long as it's not speculation and yeah, as long as it's not a repeat of what has been already asked, it's fine for you to have a supplementary. So back to mobility. Um, and there's some great work that you've been doing, the other ripple effects that you can have, but because you have such a large footprint around the country, you're dealing with making sure that the mail gets there on time or hopefully doesn't actually get there on, on time to me

because I'm on a rural community. But nevermind, um, the, um, the, what, what is the impact on, or how are you, how well are you working with partners crossing the island to, to the South Island, for example, making sure that those mobility, um, options are more decarbonized.

How, how is that, how, how influential are you in making, helping to decarbonize their mobility? Well, I mean, we, we pursue every opportunity we have, and Dave and I both have good connections and Kiwi rail. And so we, we are exploring with them whether there's further options and would we be prepared to sacrifice timeliness for the ability both at a, on a cost basis, but also something that is more friendly to the environment.

We we're looking at those opportunities all the time. And, um, it does, does help that actually we have great relationship there and we can explore that. So moving from South Island, north Island, vice versa. So we do actively explore those, those, those options. Sometimes there's a trade off in it, right?

And this is something we're interested in right now. Is there a demand out there for product that might not meet the current timeframes, but customers don't care? You know, if it took an another 24 hours, it wouldn't matter. But then we have to also marry up the cost of the handling of that.

So we are actively exploring that. It's not out there in the public, but I don't mind sharing that with you. Um, we try to do that all the time and, you know, clearly we're, we're a big player, so you would like to think we would use, you know, our position in the market to encourage those opportunity.

And if we can marry it up with a commercial opportunity that meets the needs out there, then that's even better because that might help suppliers in the community achieve an outcome that is acceptable, but at a lower cost. So yeah, we definitely, in looking at those opportunities, So one final, yeah, so, um, I'm thinking about how integrated your mobility might be.

So are you using the rail network for example, and, and the potential for electric aviation? Um, those, those options, are those, are those things that are under active Exploration? Yeah. That, that practice? Well, there, there are many things we will keep an eye on and there are other things that we will trial.

Uh, an example, for the last three or four years we've been trialing a hydrogen truck in the northern part of the country. Uh, and technically it's a great vehicle, uh, and the drivers love driving it, uh, but there's a whole lot of infrastructure and cost and needs. So I think over time those sort of initiatives will get natural momentum as infrastructure builds and, and price points come closer together.

So we see it as important that we trial those things so that when those conditions are right, we know what to expect. So that's one example. Uh, we, uh, also remember the Climate Leaders Coalition and they have subgroups and there are times with industry, uh, players to try and connect and discuss certain topics. So, uh, where it makes sense, we will get involved in those as well to see if there are industry solutions, uh, on certain aspects.

Uh, and um, yeah, we do work, uh, with our, some of our customers. IKEA we've talked about as a good example. Uh, one of the reasons that we were successful in the tender partnership is our ability to be able to offer an EV solution in Auckland. Uh, so that is what we are doing to support that also.

So there's lots of things and there's also a big challenge for all of us to continue that momentum, but mm-hmm. Uh, we, we do keep an active interest in trying to find solutions. Okay.
